subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Includes two relevant, labelled diagrams that connect directly to the smokingâban versus tax debate
Clear annotation of axes, curves and welfareâloss areas
Integrates the diagrams into the narrative with explanation of curve shifts and welfare changes
weaknesses: The first diagram misrepresents a consumption ban as a leftward supply shift rather than a demand shift
Oversimplifies the externality policy mechanism, reducing conceptual accuracy
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student supplies two accurate, well-labelled diagrams and integrates them fully into the argument, the misrepresentation of a consumption ban via a supply shift reveals a substantive conceptual error. This warrants a band-2 score under Diagrams.

strengths: Consistent and correct use of economic terms (negative externality, demerit good, specific tax, MSC/MPC, welfare loss)
Terminology is integrated fluently throughout the commentary
weaknesses: Minor notation typos (e.g. âQââââ vs. âQââââ)
Some ambiguous shorthand in curve labels (e.g. âDâ,ââ)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Terminology is used appropriately and consistently, with only minor notational slips that do not undermine conceptual clarity. This aligns with a band-2 performance.

strengths: Applies core theory of externalities, taxes, bans and price elasticity to the articleâs policy debate
Recognises welfare losses, tax revenue effects and black-market risks
weaknesses: Analysis remains superficial in places, lacking deeper quantitative development (e.g. no marginal-damage curve)
Conflates different data scopes (global WHO data vs. New Zealand figures)
Omits discussion of enforcement/administration costs and alternative policy tools
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although economic theory is applied throughout, the analysis is underdeveloped: key cost-benefit dimensions and deeper diagrammatic extensions are missing. This falls below band-2 depth, meriting a band-1 score for Application and Analysis.

strengths: Clearly and explicitly identifies âgovernment interventionâ as the key concept
Weaves the concept throughout, linking policy tools to market-failure theory and the articleâs narrative
Provides a full, clear explanation of how the concept underpins the ban vs. tax debate
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The key concept is unambiguously stated at the outset and consistently integrated with strong, article-linked explanation, fully satisfying band-3 requirements.

strengths: Offers a balanced stance on bans versus taxes, considering utility, health externalities and revenue
Judgments are logically tied to economic concepts like price elasticity and welfare loss
weaknesses: Evaluation is largely qualitative, lacking any quantitative comparison (e.g. tax revenue vs. healthcare savings)
No discussion of distributional or equity effects of the policies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Judgments are balanced but supported only by limited, qualitative reasoning. The absence of quantitative backing or deeper trade-off analysis places this evaluation at band-1.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student moves beyond description by quantifying welfare loss, contrasting short-run revenue benefits with long-run health costs, and recognising possible black-market leakage.  However, the analysis stops short of calculating external costs or exploring alternative policies in detail, so it is âappropriate but could be more developed,â matching band 2.

The student supplies two hand-drawn market-failure diagrams, each with labelled axes (P, Q), curves (MPB, MSB, MPC, âS+banâ etc.) and welfare-loss areas.  Both graphs relate directly to the policy debate in the article (smoking ban versus taxes).  Although the first diagram treats the ban as a leftward supply shift rather than a demand shift for a consumption externality, the basic geometry and labelling remain internally coherent and free from obvious factual mistakes such as inverted axes or mis-placed equilibrium points.  Hence the work fulfils the descriptor of ârelevant, accurate and correctly labelled diagram(s).â

The narrative continuously links the intervention concept to the article: explaining why the original ban was introduced, how its repeal alters incentives, and weighing intervention options.  This provides a âfull and clear explanationâ of how the key concept connects to the real-world case.

Economic theory on externalities, government intervention instruments (bans, education, indirect tax), price elasticity and black-market effects is applied to every section of the article.  While coverage is broad, depth is moderate; for example, the analysis of elasticity is brief and the positive-externality diagram is not strictly relevant to cigarettes.  Overall, theory is âapplied throughoutâ but not with exceptional depth, warranting band 2.

Judgments are supported by logical but mainly qualitative reasoningâreferences to PED, long-run healthcare costs, and welfare-loss elimination.  While sound, the reasoning could be richer (e.g., explicit cost-benefit estimates), so it meets, but does not exceed, the standard for âeffective and balanced reasoning.â

âGovernment interventionâ is stated explicitly at the outset and is entirely appropriate for a commentary on banning versus taxing tobacco.  The student weaves the concept through the discussion of market failure, command-and-control versus market-based tools, and welfare trade-offs, demonstrating clear and appropriate identification.

The commentary integrates each diagram into the argument, explicitly walking the reader through curve shifts (S1âS2+ban, D1âD2+policies), price/quantity changes, and welfare-loss triangles, and links these movements to the external costs, tax revenue and black-market concerns discussed in the article.  The explanations are complete, accurate and clearly connected to the policy issue, satisfying the requirement for a âfull and accurate explanation.â

Terms such as negative externality, demerit good, PED, specific tax, command-and-control regulation, MSB/MPB, MSC/MPC and welfare loss are employed correctly and consistently from start to finish.  There are no obvious misuses or casual phrasing, so terminology is âused appropriately throughout.â

The student articulates judgments on whether a full ban or a tax is preferable, considering utility, health externalities, fiscal revenue and black-market risks.  The stance is balanced yet not deeply critiqued with quantitative backing, fitting the description of âeffective and balanced judgmentsâ at band 2.

annotations.comment: The student clearly states ââKey Concept: Government intervention,ââ demonstrating accurate and appropriate identification aligned with the commentaryâs focus on policy choices.
Introduce a quantitative comparison of tax revenue versus projected healthcare savings to substantiate claims about long-term net benefits and enhance evaluative depth.
Including the full article title is accurate, but paraphrasing it could reduce reliance on direct quoting and free up space for original economic analysis.
Correctly noting Microeconomics aligns the commentaryâs analytical tools with the content; this clear alignment supports coherent application of theory.
This opening passage accurately paraphrases the article, but its length reduces space for theoretical application. Consider condensing factual summary to deepen economic analysis.
When summarizing policy details here, the student misses an early link to economic theory. Introduce the externality concept sooner to frame the ban versus tax debate.
Use of direct quotes is informative, but these could be better integrated into economic analysis by linking statements to market-failure theory and government tools.
Quoting the WHOâs ââeight million deathsââ adds strong empirical support. To further strengthen analysis, contrast this global data with New Zealandâspecific external cost figures.
This extended summary of group reactions could be streamlined to free space for deeper economic critique. Prioritize theory and evaluation over reiterating all quotes.
The Health Coalition Aotearoaâs normative claim could be evaluated using opportunity-cost analysis. Challenge such statements by weighing benefits against fiscal and social trade-offs.
At the end of this summary section, the student should state a concise thesis outlining their stance and planned analytical approach to guide the reader.
The introduction clearly defines government intervention and situates the articleâs reversal within market failure theory. This strong opening anchors the analysis.
When discussing negative externalities, consider adding a marginal damage (MD) curve to the diagram. This would illustrate how MSC exceeds MPC, clarifying welfare loss visually.
The mix of global WHO data and the modelled ââ80,000 livesââ saved in New Zealand risks conflating different scopes. Clarify which figures refer to which population.
Representing the ban as a leftward supply shift oversimplifies a consumption externality policy. A demand-side shift would more accurately capture reduced smoking incentives.
The discussion omits consideration of administrative costs for implementing or repealing the ban. Including these costs would deepen the real-world policy analysis.
Black-market emergence is noted, but enforcement and monitoring costs are absent. Discussing these would offer insight into the full cost of command-and-control regulation.
The supply-shift diagram is well drawn, but it misrepresents a consumption ban. For a demerit good externality, a demand-curve shift would more accurately reflect reduced smoking incentives.
The typo ââQ_{oot}ââ instead of ââQ_{opt}ââ undermines precision in mathematical notation. Correcting this will maintain clarity and rigor in the analysis.
The explanation of how price elasticity affects tax effectiveness is clear and demonstrates solid application of elasticity theory to justify policy impact.
The second diagram effectively models the demand-side shift and aligns with the policy mix of taxes and education, reinforcing the comparative analysis.
Expressions like D_{1,2} are ambiguous. Clearly distinguish between initial demand (Dâ) and post-policy demand (Dâ+policies) to improve readability.
The conclusion offers a balanced judgment on bans versus taxes and links economic objectives to social goals. This demonstrates clear, effective evaluation.
To enrich the evaluation, discuss distributional equityâwho bears the tax burden, and how a ban might disproportionately affect lower-income smokers.
Repeating the CNN headline verbatim in a section header is clear but could be paraphrased to limit direct quotations and open space for analysis.
